# How this book was made {.unnumbered}

*Health in Medicine: Nutrition* is authored by the **Department of Biochemistry & Nutrition/Metabolism Systems-based Workgroup, NUS Medicine** with AI assistance.

## The technical build

The book is a static website, built from plain-text source so that every version is diffable and
reviewable.

- **Quarto** turns the chapter source (Markdown with embedded citations and cross-references) into
  the linked, searchable site you are reading, with **KaTeX** for equations and a BibTeX
  bibliography as the single source of truth for references.
- The interactive parts, the self-assessment quizzes, the branching clinical cases, and the bespoke
  diagrams, are **Svelte** components bundled with **Vite** and hydrated into each page as
  self-contained "islands." Your progress is saved locally in your browser; there is no account and
  no login.
- The site is rebuilt and deployed automatically through a GitHub Actions pipeline on every change,
  so what you read is always the latest reviewed version.
- Figures are either original diagrams drawn for this book or openly licensed images, each credited
  to its source.

## The simulated patient

The live patient conversations in the capstone cases run on a **separate, self-hosted language
model**: an open-weights **Qwen** model served on NUS infrastructure through an OpenAI-compatible
interface. Your messages pass to a small server-side proxy that holds the key and applies the safety
guardrails, and the replies stream back in real time, tagged with a mood that drives the patient's
expression; a short rubric grades the encounter afterwards. It is a simulation for practice,
guardrailed and tested against adversarial probing, and never a substitute for a real patient or for
medical advice. If the model is unavailable, the case says so and continues.

## The authoring process

The manuscript was drafted with **Anthropic's Claude** (the Opus 4 family), run through **Claude
Code**, an agentic command-line assistant, under close human direction. Rather than one-shot
generation, the work used a deliberately adversarial, multi-agent process built to catch the failure
modes these models are known for.

1. **Research first.** For each topic, a fan-out of research agents assembled a source-backed
   dossier, and every citation was then independently re-checked by a separate "refuter" agent,
   because language models are prone to attaching a real claim to the wrong reference. Nothing
   advanced to drafting until its sources survived that check.
2. **Draft to a house style.** Each chapter was written against a fixed template and style guide:
   pathophysiology built on earlier chapters by name, a Singapore-first framing, and a "recognise
   the levers and refer" altitude for the disease chapters.
3. **Review adversarially.** Separate agents then critiqued each draft along independent dimensions,
   clinical accuracy against the dossier, house style, the quiz and case design, and the figures,
   with the findings triaged and fixed.
4. **Verify mechanically.** Deterministic checks rendered the whole book, confirmed that no asset or
   citation was missing, linted the cases, and opened the interactive pages in a real browser to
   confirm they worked, before anything was committed.

The resulting draft was then reviewed and edited by medical educators.

## A note to the reader

This is an educational resource, not clinical guidance. Medicine and its evidence change over time;
check against current local guidelines before acting on anything here.
